text:
In 2018, the U.S. Congress passed the Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act, known as S. 2155. The law raised the threshold at which banks faced enhanced prudential standards under Dodd-Frank from $50 billion in assets to $250 billion. SVB was among the banks that supported that change. The direct result: SVB was not required to run formal stress tests against interest rate scenarios under the Dodd-Frank Act Stress Test framework. The very risk that destroyed it was the risk regulators were no longer required to formally test.

SVB also entered 2022 without a Chief Risk Officer. Laura Izurieta departed in April 2022. Her replacement, Kim Olson, was not named until January 2023. During those eight months, the Federal Reserve raised its benchmark rate from 0.25 percent to 4.5 percent. There was no executive formally accountable for the portfolio's duration risk as that risk compounded.

On March 10, 2023, California's Department of Financial Protection and Innovation closed Silicon Valley Bank and appointed the FDIC as receiver. It was the second-largest bank failure in U.S. history by assets, behind only Washington Mutual in 2008. On March 12, the FDIC invoked a systemic risk exception, guaranteeing all deposits. That decision reflected an uncomfortable number from the 2022 10-K: approximately 93.8 percent of SVB's deposits exceeded the $250,000 FDIC insurance limit.

The FDIC's post-mortem, published April 28, 2023, called SVB's failure a textbook case of mismanagement. The Federal Reserve's own review, published the same day, found that supervisors had issued multiple findings against SVB that management had not fully remediated. The bank was not ambushed. It was warned, repeatedly, and the warnings were not acted on.

First Citizens BancShares acquired SVB's deposits and loans from the FDIC on March 27, 2023, at a discount the FDIC estimated would cost the Deposit Insurance Fund approximately $20 billion.

The standard telling of the SVB story is a story about a social media panic and a VC herd. That story is not wrong. It is incomplete. A bank holding $91 billion in bonds with $15 billion in unrealized losses, no Chief Risk Officer, a deposit base that was 94 percent uninsured, and a regulatory exemption from the stress tests that would have caught this: that bank was not one provocative tweet away from stability. It was one honest accounting update away from a run. The March 8 8-K was that update. And the run came because the math had been true for months before anyone said it out loud.

edited_script.title: SVB: The $42B Run - Part 2
edited_script.description: The regulatory backstory, missing CRO, FDIC seizure, and the contrarian reframe: the run was rational.
